checks.question: The H100 SXM5 has 80 GB of HBM3 global memory with 3.35 TB/s bandwidth (latency ~200-800 cycles), 50 MB of L2 cache (~200 cycles), and up to 228 KB of shared memory per SM (~20-30 cycles). A kernel reads a 4-byte value from global memory. If the value is in the L2 cache, how much faster is the access compared to an uncached global memory access?
checks.explanation: L2 cache latency is approximately 200 cycles, while uncached global memory (HBM3) latency ranges from 200 to 800 cycles depending on access pattern and contention. So an L2 hit is roughly 2-4x faster than a full HBM access in the worst case. Note the distinction: shared memory at ~20-30 cycles would be the ~10x improvement option, but the question asks about L2 cache specifically. The key insight is that L2 cache helps most with repeated accesses to the same data -- the first access still pays the full HBM latency, but subsequent accesses to the same cache line are served from L2 at ~200 cycles.

checks.question: A warp of 32 threads accesses a float32 array where thread $i$ reads `data[i * 2]`. Each float32 is 4 bytes, so thread $i$ accesses byte address $8i$. How many 128-byte memory transactions are required to serve this warp?
options: 2 transactions (addresses span 0 to 248, requiring two 128-byte cache lines)
32 transactions (each thread requires its own transaction)
checks.explanation: Thread 0 accesses byte 0, thread 1 accesses byte 8, ..., thread 15 accesses byte 120 (all within the first 128-byte cache line, bytes 0-127). Thread 16 accesses byte 128, ..., thread 31 accesses byte 248 (all within the second cache line, bytes 128-255). So exactly 2 transactions are needed. Note that only 32 x 4 = 128 bytes of useful data are loaded, but 256 bytes are transferred (two 128-byte lines). Bandwidth utilization is 50%. Compare this to stride-1 access (thread $i$ reads `data[i]`), which would need only 1 transaction with 100% utilization.

checks.question: Shared memory has 32 banks with a 4-byte stride. A kernel declares `__shared__ float tile[32][32]` and 32 threads simultaneously read `tile[i][col]` for a fixed column `col` (thread $i$ reads row $i$). What is the severity of the bank conflict, and how does padding to `float tile[32][33]` fix it?
options: No conflict occurs because each thread reads from a different row
2-way conflict; padding reduces it to no conflict by changing the stride from 32 to 33

32-way conflict (all threads hit the same bank because stride 32 maps every row to bank 0); padding changes the stride to 33, and since gcd(33, 32) = 1, consecutive rows map to consecutive banks, eliminating all conflicts
checks.explanation: In `tile[32][32]`, element `tile[i][col]` is at byte offset $(32i + \text{col}) \times 4$. The bank is $(32i + \text{col}) \bmod 32 = \text{col} \bmod 32 = \text{col}$ for all $i$. Every thread hits the same bank -- a 32-way conflict, requiring 32 serial transactions. With padding to `tile[32][33]`, element `tile[i][col]` is at byte offset $(33i + \text{col}) \times 4$. The bank is $(33i + \text{col}) \bmod 32$. Since $\gcd(33, 32) = 1$, the values $33i \bmod 32$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, 31$ produce all 32 distinct residues. Each thread hits a different bank: zero conflicts. Cost: 3% extra shared memory (33 vs 32 elements per row). Benefit: 32x improvement in this access pattern.

checks.question: A kernel on the H100 uses 128 threads per block, 48 registers per thread, and 8 KB of shared memory per block. The H100 SM supports max 2,048 threads, max 32 blocks, 65,536 registers, and 228 KB shared memory. What is the occupancy, and what is the bottleneck?
options: 62.5% occupancy; bottleneck is registers (48 regs x 128 threads = 6,144 per block, $\lfloor 65536 / 6144 \rfloor = 10$ blocks, giving 40 warps out of 64 max)
50% occupancy; bottleneck is shared memory (8 KB per block limits to 28 blocks)
25% occupancy; bottleneck is the block size limit of 32
checks.explanation: Thread limit: $\lfloor 2048 / 128 \rfloor = 16$ blocks (within the 32-block max). Register limit: $128 \times 48 = 6{,}144$ regs per block. $\lfloor 65{,}536 / 6{,}144 \rfloor = 10$ blocks. Shared memory limit: $\lfloor 228 / 8 \rfloor = 28$ blocks. Active blocks = $\min(16, 10, 28, 32) = 10$, limited by registers. Active warps = $10 \times (128/32) = 40$. Occupancy = $40/64 = 62.5\%$. Registers are the bottleneck. To improve: reduce register usage to 32 per thread, giving $128 \times 32 = 4{,}096$ regs/block, and $\lfloor 65{,}536 / 4{,}096 \rfloor = 16$ blocks. That matches the thread limit, yielding $16 \times 4 = 64$ warps = 100% occupancy. But reducing registers may cause spills to local memory, so the trade-off must be profiled.
